content:
# Exodus Chapter 21 -- The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~44v-45r (PDF pp. 87-88); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 63-64 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_021_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_021_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_021_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_021_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

> **MANUSCRIPT NOTE -- THE BOOK OF THE COVENANT BEGINS (SEFER HA-BERIT):** The Leningrad Codex presents Exodus 21 in standard prose column format with full Masoretic vocalization and cantillation marks. This chapter opens the legal corpus known as the Book of the Covenant (*Sefer ha-Berit*, cf. 24:7), extending through 23:33. It constitutes the oldest collection of Israelite case law, transitioning from the apodictic ("you shall / you shall not") commands of the Decalogue (ch. 20) to casuistic ("if/when a man does X, then Y") jurisprudence. The structure is tripartite: laws concerning Hebrew slaves and their rights (vv. 1-11), laws concerning violence, capital offenses, and bodily injury including the *lex talionis* (vv. 12-27), and laws concerning liability for dangerous animals and property damage (vv. 28-36). The parallels with the Code of Hammurabi, the Laws of Eshnunna, and the Hittite Laws are extensive, though the Israelite laws differ crucially in the equal valuation of human life regardless of social class and in the theological grounding of law in covenant relationship with YHWH. The Vaticanus text (pp. 63-64) presents the LXX rendering in continuous uncial script across three columns per page.

---

## The Ordinances Introduced (v. 1)

**21:1** And these are the ordinances which you shall set before them: [fn1]

---

## Laws Concerning Hebrew Slaves (vv. 2-6)

**21:2** When you buy a Hebrew slave, six years he shall serve, and in the seventh he shall go out free, for nothing. [fn2]

**21:3** If he comes in alone, he shall go out alone. If he is the husband of a wife, then his wife shall go out with him. [fn3]

**21:4** If his master gives him a wife and she bears him sons or daughters, the wife and her children shall belong to her master, and he shall go out alone. [fn4]

**21:5** And if the slave surely says, "I love my master, my wife, and my children; I will not go out free" -- [fn5]

**21:6** then his master shall bring him to ha-Elohim, and he shall bring him to the door or to the doorpost, and his master shall pierce his ear with an awl, and he shall serve him forever. [fn6]

---

## Laws Concerning Female Servants (vv. 7-11)

**21:7** And when a man sells his daughter as a female servant, she shall not go out as the male servants go out. [fn7]

**21:8** If she is displeasing in the eyes of her master who designated her for himself, then he shall let her be redeemed. He shall have no authority to sell her to a foreign people, since he has dealt treacherously with her. [fn8]

**21:9** And if he designates her for his son, he shall deal with her according to the ordinance of daughters. [fn9]

**21:10** If he takes another wife for himself, her food, her clothing, and her conjugal rights he shall not diminish. [fn10]

**21:11** And if these three things he does not do for her, then she shall go out for nothing, without silver. [fn11]

---

## Capital Offenses (vv. 12-17)

**21:12** One who strikes a man so that he dies shall surely be put to death. [fn12]

**21:13** And he who did not lie in wait, but God caused it to happen to his hand, then I will appoint for you a place to which he may flee. [fn13]

**21:14** And if a man acts presumptuously against his neighbor to kill him by cunning, from my altar you shall take him to die. [fn14]

**21:15** And one who strikes his father or his mother shall surely be put to death. [fn15]

**21:16** And one who steals a man, whether he has sold him or he is found in his hand, shall surely be put to death. [fn16]

**21:17** And one who curses his father or his mother shall surely be put to death. [fn17]

---

## Laws Concerning Bodily Injury (vv. 18-21)

**21:18** And when men quarrel and one strikes his neighbor with a stone or with a fist, and he does not die but falls to his bed -- [fn18]

**21:19** if he rises and walks about outside upon his staff, then the one who struck him shall be cleared; only he shall pay for his lost time and shall surely have him healed. [fn19]

**21:20** And when a man strikes his male servant or his female servant with a rod and the servant dies under his hand, he shall surely be avenged. [fn20]

**21:21** However, if the servant survives a day or two, he shall not be avenged, for he is his property. [fn21]

---

## Injury to a Pregnant Woman and the Lex Talionis (vv. 22-25)

**21:22** And when men struggle and they strike a pregnant woman and her children come out, but there is no harm, he shall surely be fined as the husband of the woman lays upon him, and he shall pay as the judges determine. [fn22]

**21:23** But if there is harm, then you shall give life for life, [fn23]

**21:24** eye for eye, tooth for tooth, hand for hand, foot for foot,

**21:25** burn for burn, wound for wound, stripe for stripe.

---

## Injury to a Servant's Body (vv. 26-27)

**21:26** And when a man strikes the eye of his male servant or the eye of his female servant and destroys it, he shall let him go free on account of his eye. [fn26]

**21:27** And if he knocks out the tooth of his male servant or the tooth of his female servant, he shall let him go free on account of his tooth. [fn27]

---

## The Goring Ox (vv. 28-32)

**21:28** And when an ox gores a man or a woman and they die, the ox shall surely be stoned and its flesh shall not be eaten; but the owner of the ox shall be clear. [fn28]

**21:29** But if the ox was a goring ox from time past, and its owner has been warned but has not guarded it, and it kills a man or a woman, the ox shall be stoned, and also its owner shall be put to death. [fn29]

**21:30** If a ransom is laid upon him, then he shall give the redemption price of his life according to all that is laid upon him. [fn30]

**21:31** Whether it gores a son or gores a daughter, according to this ordinance it shall be done to him. [fn31]

**21:32** If the ox gores a male servant or a female servant, he shall give thirty shekels of silver to their master, and the ox shall be stoned. [fn32]

---

## Laws Concerning Property Damage (vv. 33-36)

**21:33** And when a man opens a pit, or when a man digs a pit and does not cover it, and an ox or a donkey falls into it -- [fn33]

**21:34** the owner of the pit shall make restitution; he shall pay silver to the owner of the animal, and the dead animal shall be his. [fn34]

**21:35** And when a man's ox strikes the ox of his neighbor and it dies, then they shall sell the living ox and divide its price, and also the dead one they shall divide. [fn35]

**21:36** Or if it was known that the ox was a goring ox from time past, and its owner has not guarded it, he shall surely pay ox for ox, and the dead one shall be his. [fn36]

---

> **[fn1] v.1 -- *Ve'elleh hammishpatim asher tasim lifneihem*: "And these are the ordinances which you shall set before them"** -- The conjunction *ve'elleh* ("and these") connects the legal corpus directly to the Decalogue -- these laws are not a separate topic but an extension and elaboration of the Ten Words. EMET renders "And these are the judgments which you shall set before them." LOGOS renders "And these are the ordinances that you shall set before them." PNEUMA renders "These are the legal rulings you are to set before them." The consensus follows LOGOS for *mishpatim* as "ordinances" -- the standard technical English term for casuistic law -- and preserves the opening conjunction "And" per binding convention (PNEUMA drops it). EMET's "judgments" is defensible but narrower in English connotation. PNEUMA's "legal rulings" is interpretive and drops the conjunction.

> **[fn2] v.2 -- *Ki tiqneh eved ivri*: "When you buy a Hebrew slave"** -- The Hebrew *eved* ("slave, servant") denotes a debt-bondsman, not a chattel slave in the Greco-Roman sense. EMET renders "slave." LOGOS renders "servant." PNEUMA renders "servant." The consensus follows EMET -- "slave" -- because *eved ivri* in its legal context denotes a person in bondage, and "servant" may soften the institution beyond what the Hebrew intends. The LXX's use of *pais* ("boy, servant") rather than *doulos* ("slave") reflects Hellenistic sensibility, not the Hebrew register. The release formula *yetse la-khofshi khinnam* ("he shall go out free, for nothing") echoes the exodus itself -- the same verb *yatsa* used for Israel's liberation from Egypt.

> **[fn3] v.3 -- *Be-gappo*: "alone" or "by himself"** -- The rare word *gap* ("body, self") with the preposition *be-* means "in his body-alone." EMET renders "alone." LOGOS renders "by himself." PNEUMA renders "single." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS with "alone" -- the most natural English rendering. PNEUMA's "single" introduces a modern marital-status connotation absent from the Hebrew.

> **[fn4] v.4 -- The wife and children remain with the master** -- All three translators agree on substance. EMET renders *tihyeh la-adoneiha* as "shall be her master's." LOGOS renders "shall belong to her master." PNEUMA renders "belong to the master." The consensus follows LOGOS -- "shall belong to her master" -- which makes the possessive relationship explicit in natural English while preserving the legal force of the Hebrew.

> **[fn5] v.5 -- *Amor yomar*: the infinitive absolute construction** -- EMET renders "will surely say." LOGOS renders "plainly says." PNEUMA renders "openly declares." The consensus follows EMET -- "surely says" -- preserving the infinitive absolute doubling per binding convention. The doubled construction *amor yomar* emphasizes the deliberateness of the declaration. LOGOS's "plainly" and PNEUMA's "openly" are interpretive glosses.

> **[fn6] v.6 -- *El-ha-Elohim*: "to God" or "to the judges"** -- This is a celebrated crux. EMET renders "to ha-Elohim [God/the judges]." LOGOS renders "to God." PNEUMA renders "before God." The consensus retains "ha-Elohim" untranslated -- preserving the deliberate ambiguity of the Hebrew, which can mean "God," "the judges," or both simultaneously. The LXX's *pros to kriterion tou theou* ("to the judgment-seat of God") combines both senses. The term *le-olam* ("forever") is retained at face value; the rabbinic limitation to the Jubilee year (Lev 25:40) is a later harmonization.

> **[fn7] v.7 -- *Le-amah*: "female servant" or "female slave"** -- EMET renders "female-slave." LOGOS renders "female servant." PNEUMA renders "servant." The consensus follows LOGOS -- "female servant" -- because the *amah* has a distinct legal status from the *eved*: she is implicitly destined for marriage, not merely labor. The distinction *lo tetse ke-tset ha-avadim* ("she shall not go out as the male servants go out") is preserved across all three.

> **[fn8] v.8 -- *Ye'adah* and the Qere/Ketiv variant** -- The Qere reads *lo* ("for himself") = the master designated her as his wife. The Ketiv reads *lo* ("not") = the master failed to designate her. EMET notes both readings. LOGOS follows the Qere: "who designated her for himself." PNEUMA follows the Qere: "who had designated her for himself." The consensus follows the Qere with LOGOS's phrasing. The term *be-vigdo* ("in his dealing treacherously") uses the root *bagad*, used elsewhere for marital infidelity and covenant violation. EMET renders "treacherously." LOGOS renders "deceitfully." PNEUMA renders "treacherously." The consensus follows EMET/PNEUMA -- "treacherously" -- as the standard rendering of *bagad*.

> **[fn9] v.9 -- *Ke-mishpat ha-banot*: "according to the ordinance of daughters"** -- All three translators agree on substance. EMET renders "according to the judgment of the daughters." LOGOS renders "according to the ordinance of daughters." PNEUMA renders "with the full rights of a daughter." The consensus follows LOGOS -- "according to the ordinance of daughters" -- which preserves *mishpat* consistently as "ordinance" (as in v. 1). PNEUMA's "full rights" is interpretive.

> **[fn10] v.10 -- *She'erah, kesutah, ve-onatah*: the three marital obligations** -- The trio of food, clothing, and conjugal rights constitutes the minimum obligations of marriage. All three translators agree on this rendering. EMET and LOGOS preserve the Hebrew word order (object before verb); PNEUMA rearranges into standard English order. The consensus preserves the Hebrew emphatic word order: "her food, her clothing, and her conjugal rights he shall not diminish."

> **[fn11] v.11 -- *Khinnam ein kasef*: "for nothing, without silver"** -- EMET renders "for nothing, without silver." LOGOS renders "for nothing, without payment of money." PNEUMA renders "without paying anything." The consensus follows EMET -- "for nothing, without silver" -- preserving the double emphasis of the Hebrew and the specific term *kesef* ("silver"), which LOGOS paraphrases as "payment of money" and PNEUMA condenses entirely.

> **[fn12] v.12 -- *Mot yumat*: the death penalty formula** -- The infinitive absolute + hophal imperfect construction: "dying he shall be put to death" = "he shall surely be put to death." EMET renders "he shall surely be put to death." LOGOS renders "shall surely be put to death." PNEUMA renders "must be put to death." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS, preserving the "surely" that reflects the doubling construction. PNEUMA's "must" loses the emphatic force.

> **[fn13] v.13 -- *Ve-ha-Elohim innah le-yado*: "God caused it to happen to his hand"** -- EMET renders "ha-Elohim caused [it] to happen to his hand." LOGOS renders "God let it happen to his hand." PNEUMA renders "God let it happen by chance." The consensus follows LOGOS -- "God caused it to happen to his hand" -- using "caused" (from EMET, closer to the piel *innah*) with LOGOS's natural phrasing. PNEUMA's addition of "by chance" imports a concept absent from the Hebrew, which attributes the event to divine causation, not randomness.

> **[fn14] v.14 -- *Yazid...be-ormah*: "acts presumptuously...by cunning"** -- EMET renders "acts presumptuously...with cunning." LOGOS renders "acts presumptuously...by cunning." PNEUMA renders "schemes deliberately...by treachery." The consensus follows LOGOS -- "acts presumptuously...by cunning." PNEUMA's "schemes deliberately" loses the specific Hebrew *yazid* (from *zid*, "to boil over, be insolent") and *ormah* (the same root as the serpent's "craftiness" in Gen 3:1).

> **[fn15] v.15 -- *Aviv ve-immo*: "his father or his mother"** -- The Hebrew conjunction *ve-* ("and") is used, but the legal sense is "or" (either parent). EMET renders "his father or his mother" (interpreting). LOGOS renders "his father or his mother." PNEUMA renders "their father or mother." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS. PNEUMA's use of "their" (gender-neutral plural) departs from the Hebrew singular.

> **[fn16] v.16 -- *Ve-gonev ish*: "one who steals a man"** -- EMET renders "one who steals a man and sells him, and he is found in his hand." LOGOS renders "he who kidnaps a man, whether he sells him or he is found in his hand." PNEUMA renders "anyone who kidnaps a person -- whether they have sold the victim or the victim is still in their possession." The consensus follows a blend: "one who steals a man" preserves *ganav* ("steal") rather than interpreting as "kidnaps" (LOGOS/PNEUMA), while "whether he has sold him or he is found in his hand" clarifies the Hebrew syntax.

> **[fn17] v.17 -- *U-meqallel*: "curses"** -- The piel of *qalal* ("to make light, curse, revile") is the precise antonym of *kavad* ("to honor, make heavy") from the Fifth Commandment. All three translators agree: "curses." The consensus preserves this unanimity.

> **[fn18] v.18 -- *Be-even o ve-egrof*: "with a stone or with a fist"** -- All three agree on "stone" and "fist." EMET renders "falls to the bed." LOGOS renders "takes to his bed." PNEUMA renders "is confined to bed." The consensus follows EMET -- "falls to his bed" -- preserving the Hebrew verb *nafal* ("to fall"), which LOGOS and PNEUMA paraphrase.

> **[fn19] v.19 -- *Shivto yitten ve-rappo yerappeh*: "his lost time he shall pay and healing he shall heal"** -- EMET renders "his sitting idle he shall pay and he shall surely cause him to be healed." LOGOS renders "he shall pay for his loss of time and shall surely have him healed." PNEUMA renders "they must pay for the victim's lost time and ensure they are fully healed." The consensus follows LOGOS -- "he shall pay for his lost time and shall surely have him healed" -- preserving the infinitive absolute doubling ("surely...healed") while using natural English. EMET's "sitting idle" is overly literal for *shivto*; PNEUMA drops the emphatic construction.

> **[fn20] v.20 -- *Naqom yinnaqem*: "he shall surely be avenged"** -- The infinitive absolute + niphal imperfect: "avenging he shall be avenged." EMET renders "he shall surely be avenged." LOGOS renders "he shall surely be avenged." PNEUMA renders "the master must be punished." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS. PNEUMA's "punished" loses the specific Hebrew *naqam* ("to avenge") and its theological weight -- the slave's death demands vengeance, not merely administrative punishment.

> **[fn21] v.21 -- *Ki khaspo hu*: "for he is his property"** -- EMET renders "for he is his silver." LOGOS renders "for he is his property." PNEUMA renders "since the servant is his property." The consensus follows LOGOS -- "for he is his property" -- as a rendering that preserves the legal force while being intelligible in English. EMET's "his silver" is the more literal rendering of *kaspo* ("his silver/money") but may confuse modern readers. This verse remains among the most ethically difficult in the chapter; the tension between the slave's personhood (v. 20) and property-status (v. 21) is unresolved in the text.

> **[fn22] v.22 -- *Ve-yatse'u yeladeiha*: "her children come out"** -- All three translators preserve the plural *yeladeiha* ("her children"). EMET and LOGOS render literally. PNEUMA adds "prematurely," which is contextually implied but not stated in the Hebrew. The term *ason* ("harm") is rare and debated -- it may refer to death specifically or to any serious bodily harm. The LXX introduces a formed/unformed fetus distinction (*exeikonismenon*) absent from the MT.

> **[fn23] v.23 -- *Nefesh takhat nefesh*: the lex talionis** -- The principle of proportional justice. The preposition *takhat* ("in place of, instead of") is the key: exact equivalence. All three translators agree on "life for life, eye for eye, tooth for tooth..." The consensus preserves the staccato, verbless rhythm of the Hebrew. Whether this was literally enforced or represents a principle of monetary compensation is debated (Bava Qamma 83b-84a).

> **[fn26] v.26 -- *La-khofshi yeshallekhennu*: "he shall let him go free"** -- All three translators agree on substance. EMET renders "he shall send him free." LOGOS renders "he shall let him go free." PNEUMA renders "the master must let the servant go free." The consensus follows LOGOS -- "he shall let him go free" -- as the most natural English. The *takhat* ("on account of") connects this to the lex talionis: the slave receives freedom as the equivalent of the lost body part.

> **[fn27] v.27 -- *Yappil*: "knocks out"** -- The hiphil of *nafal* ("to cause to fall") = "knocks out." All three translators agree. The rabbinic tradition (Qiddushin 24a-b) extends the principle from eye and tooth to all twenty-four "extremities" of the body.

> **[fn28] v.28 -- *Saqol yissaqel*: "the ox shall surely be stoned"** -- The infinitive absolute construction. The ox is executed as if it were a murderer; its flesh is taboo. EMET renders "the ox shall surely be stoned." LOGOS renders "the ox shall surely be stoned." PNEUMA renders "the ox must be stoned." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS, preserving the "surely" doubling.

> **[fn29] v.29 -- *Mi-temol shilshom*: "from time past"** -- Literally "from yesterday and the day before." EMET renders "from yesterday and the day before." LOGOS renders "in times past." PNEUMA renders "has had a habit of goring." The consensus renders "from time past" -- close to LOGOS while preserving the idiom. EMET is overly literal; PNEUMA paraphrases too freely. The term *naggakh* (qattal intensive form) indicates habitual action -- a known pattern of dangerous behavior.

> **[fn30] v.30 -- *Kofer...pidyon*: "ransom...redemption price"** -- EMET distinguishes the two terms: *kofer* ("ransom") and *pidyon* ("redemption-price"). LOGOS uses "ransom" and "redemption price." PNEUMA renders simply "ransom." The consensus preserves both terms with LOGOS's phrasing. The root *k-p-r* ("to cover, atone") connects this legal provision to the broader system of atonement. The permission to commute the death penalty here contrasts with Numbers 35:31, which prohibits accepting a *kofer* for a direct murderer.

> **[fn31] v.31 -- Equal application regardless of victim's gender** -- All three agree on substance. EMET renders "whether a son it gores or a daughter it gores." LOGOS renders "whether it gores a son or gores a daughter." PNEUMA renders "whether the ox gores someone's son or someone's daughter." The consensus follows LOGOS for natural English while preserving the Hebrew's emphatic repetition of the verb.

> **[fn32] v.32 -- *Sheloshim sheqalim*: "thirty shekels of silver"** -- The fixed compensation for a slave killed by a goring ox. This amount reappears in Zechariah 11:12-13 as the contemptible price of the shepherd and in Matthew 26:15 as the price of Judas's betrayal. All three translators agree. EMET renders "silver -- thirty sheqels." LOGOS renders "thirty shekels of silver." PNEUMA renders "thirty shekels of silver." The consensus follows LOGOS/PNEUMA's word order.

> **[fn33] v.33 -- *Bor*: "pit"** -- Two scenarios: opening an existing pit (*yiftakh*) and digging a new one (*yikhreh*). All three agree on "pit." The negligence is failing to cover it (*ve-lo yekhassennu*).

> **[fn34] v.34 -- *Yeshallem*: "shall make restitution"** -- The piel of *shalam* ("to make whole") -- from the same root as *shalom*. EMET renders "shall pay." LOGOS renders "shall make restitution." PNEUMA renders "must make restitution." The consensus follows LOGOS -- "shall make restitution" -- capturing the *shalom*-restoring force of the piel stem.

> **[fn35] v.35 -- *Ve-khatsu et-kaspo*: "divide its price"** -- EMET renders "divide its silver." LOGOS renders "divide its price." PNEUMA renders "divide the money equally." The consensus follows LOGOS -- "divide its price" -- rendering *kaspo* functionally while avoiding PNEUMA's addition of "equally" (implied but not stated).

> **[fn36] v.36 -- *Shallem yeshallem shor takhat ha-shor*: "he shall surely pay ox for ox"** -- The infinitive absolute emphasizes the obligation of full restitution. When the owner knew the animal was dangerous and failed to restrain it, shared loss (v. 35) gives way to full liability. All three translators agree. The *takhat* ("in place of") is the same talionic preposition from vv. 23-25, now applied to property.

---

## Translator's Notes

### Structural Note

Exodus 21 is the first chapter of the Book of the Covenant (*Sefer ha-Berit*, cf. 24:7), the oldest law collection in the Hebrew Bible. The chapter moves from laws governing persons (vv. 2-27) to laws governing property (vv. 28-36). Within the person-laws, it progresses from servitude (vv. 2-11) through capital offenses (vv. 12-17) to bodily injury (vv. 18-27), before turning to animal and property cases (vv. 28-36). The casuistic ("if...then") form that dominates this chapter stands in sharp contrast to the apodictic ("you shall / you shall not") form of the Decalogue. The two forms are complementary: the Decalogue establishes moral and theological principles; the *mishpatim* apply those principles to the realities of communal life. Together they form a single legal revelation given by the same God at the same mountain.

### Key Decisions

**"Ordinances" for *mishpatim* (v. 1):** LOGOS's "ordinances" was preferred over EMET's "judgments" and PNEUMA's "legal rulings." The term "ordinances" is the standard English equivalent for casuistic legal rulings and appears consistently in the scholarly literature on ancient Israelite law.

**"Slave" vs. "servant" for *eved* (v. 2):** EMET's "slave" was preferred over LOGOS/PNEUMA's "servant." While the *eved ivri* is a debt-bondsman rather than a chattel slave, "servant" may minimize the reality of bondage that the text both describes and regulates. The LXX's preference for *pais* over *doulos* reflects Hellenistic sensibility, not the Hebrew register.

**"Ha-Elohim" retained (v. 6):** The consensus retains the Hebrew term untranslated -- a departure from all three translators -- because the deliberate ambiguity (God? judges? both?) is theologically significant. The LXX's *pros to kriterion tou theou* ("to the judgment-seat of God") confirms that both senses were understood simultaneously in antiquity.

**"Property" for *kaspo* (v. 21):** LOGOS's "property" was preferred over EMET's strictly literal "his silver." The Hebrew *kaspo hu* ("he is his silver") uses *kesef* metonymically for financial investment/property. EMET's rendering preserves the Hebrew metaphor but may obscure the legal point for English readers.

**"From time past" for *mi-temol shilshom* (vv. 29, 36):** A rendering between EMET's overly literal "from yesterday and the day before" and PNEUMA's paraphrase "has had a habit." LOGOS's "in times past" was close; the consensus slightly adjusts to "from time past" to preserve the Hebrew idiom's sense of established prior behavior.

### Vaticanus (LXX) Divergences

The Vaticanus text of Exodus 21 preserves several significant divergences from the MT: (1) At v. 2, *eved ivri* becomes *paida Hebraion* ("a Hebrew servant"), using *pais* rather than *doulos*. (2) At v. 6, *el-ha-Elohim* is rendered *pros to kriterion tou theou* ("to the judgment-seat of God"), combining divine and judicial senses. (3) At v. 7, the LXX reads *hai doulai* ("the female slaves") where the MT has *ha-avadim* (masculine, "the male slaves"). (4) At v. 13, *innah le-yado* becomes *paredoken eis tas cheiras autou* ("delivered into his hands"), strengthening the divine-providence reading. (5) At vv. 22-23, the most significant divergence: the LXX introduces a formed/unformed fetus distinction (*exeikonismenon* / *me exeikonismenon*) absent from the MT, which profoundly influenced early Christian discussions of ensoulment and fetal personhood. (6) At v. 32, the thirty shekels become *triakonta didrachma* ("thirty double-drachmas"). (7) The verse ordering in vv. 15-17 differs, with the cursing-of-parents law placed before the kidnapping law.

---

*Translated from the Leningrad Codex (B19A) with reference to Codex Vaticanus (LXX). All transliterations follow standard academic conventions. Hebrew text follows the Masoretic vocalization.*
